paragraphs: Cook Dependency + Kitchen Capability Persona Specification
DOC-16 | Version 1.0 | Meal Planning RE Technical Docs v1
1. Purpose and Scope
Defines how cook availability, cook skill, need for instructions, household time pressure, kitchen capability, and batch-cooking behavior influence meal classes and dish ranking.

This document is part of the canonical 28-document technical system. It should be read in the order defined in DOC-00. It is written for an AI coding agent that must implement the agreed recommendation engine exactly, without inventing a competing architecture.
2. Applicable Build Series
3. Core Implementation Invariants
Always build a household profile before generating any plan.
Always map user-facing cohort answers into backend personas and overlays; do not expose all backend personas in onboarding.
Always generate a 7-day class-first plan before selecting dishes.
Always keep primary family meal classes separate from member-specific add-on components.
Never allow kid, elderly, pregnancy, postpartum, diabetic, or recovery add-on classes to replace the main family meal unless the household itself has only that member segment.
Always apply hard constraints before scoring: diet type, allergies, Jain/fasting rules, and Never list.
Always use home state and current city as separate signals; do not collapse them into one location field.
Always use class-to-dish mapping keyed by meal_class_code; never mix dishes from unrelated meal classes.
Use v3 workbook IDs as canonical seed IDs unless a later approved source-data version supersedes them.
4. Why Cook Capability Matters

A technically perfect meal recommendation fails if the household cannot execute it. Many urban Indian homes rely on cooks with different skill levels: skilled cook, basic cook, cook needing constant instruction, no cook, working woman managing cooking, elderly cook, or batch-prep household.
5. Cook Personas
6. Class Filtering by Complexity

Meal classes have cook_complexity and cook_time-like attributes in the taxonomy. Weekday plans should demote high-complexity classes for no-cook/basic-cook/high-time-pressure households. Weekend can restore some complexity if cohort allows.
7. Instruction Quality Requirements
For assisted cook households, dish cards should include short cook instructions, not just recipe name.
For basic cooks, prefer dishes with repeatable base gravies, pressure cooker dal, simple sabzi, and familiar breakfast classes.
For no-cook households, use classes that can be assembled or outsourced, but still map to meal classes.
For batch-cook households, allow dal/sabzi bases that can carry forward into next day variations.
8. Implementation Fields
9. AI Coding Agent Instructions
Read DOC-00 first to understand build sequence and document dependencies.
Use this document when implementing: BUILD-02, BUILD-04.
Treat all IDs in source workbook v3 as canonical seed data identifiers.
When logic and data conflict, follow the rule hierarchy: hard constraints > add-on separation > weekly class matrix > state/city overlay > dish scoring > randomization.
Do not create new meal class codes, persona codes, or enum values in application code. Additions must go through DOC-27 Admin CMS and DOC-28 governance.
Write unit tests based on the acceptance criteria in this document and DOC-25.
10. Acceptance Criteria
Weekday plans are executable for cook capability.
Complex dishes are not suggested to no-cook/basic-cook households by default.
Cook-dependent users get classes and dishes with appropriate instruction complexity.
11. Change Log

Only moderate complexity if user available.
Quick assembly, leftovers, ready components, low-cook dishes.

Low time weekday, batch-cook, pressure cooker, one-pot.
More variety if weekend time available.
